questions.question: What electrocardiographic finding is characteristic of left ventricular hypertrophy due to aortic stenosis?
questions.explanation: The Sokolow-Lyon index (S in V1 + R in V5 or V6 > 35 mm) is a classic criterion of left ventricular hypertrophy on the ECG, frequently positive in severe aortic stenosis.

questions.question: What is the most common electrical axis deviation in left ventricular hypertrophy due to aortic stenosis?
questions.explanation: Left ventricular hypertrophy usually deviates the electrical axis to the left (between -30° and -90°), reflecting the increase in muscle mass of the left ventricle.

questions.question: What T wave pattern is typically associated with left ventricular hypertrophy due to aortic stenosis?
options: Inverted T wave in left precordials
questions.explanation: In left ventricular hypertrophy with systolic overload (aortic stenosis), inversion of the T wave in left precordial leads (V5-V6) is common due to altered repolarization.

questions.question: What electrocardiographic criterion uses the sum of R waves in V5 and S waves in V1 for left ventricular hypertrophy?
questions.explanation: The Sokolow-Lyon index measures S in V1 + R in V5 or V6 > 35 mm, while the Cornell criterion uses R in aVL + S in V3.

questions.question: Which lead most frequently shows tall R waves in left ventricular hypertrophy due to aortic stenosis?
questions.explanation: In left ventricular hypertrophy, the R wave is usually increased in the left precordial leads (V5-V6) due to the increase in the left ventricular vector.

questions.question: What ST segment alteration is observed in severe left ventricular hypertrophy due to aortic stenosis?
questions.explanation: Systolic overload of the left ventricle produces a strain pattern with depression of the ST segment in left precordial leads (V5-V6), accompanied by inversion of the T wave.
